hub_subtitle: From hesitant communicator to confident global business leader

lesson.menu_desc: Milton steps in front of an investor to win backing for the next stage of the export business. Learn to frame what makes the company special with emphasis (cleft) sentences, present the numbers, handle a sharp question and ask for a clear commitment — equity, a stake, traction, a backer, a valuation, returns — and close a confident pitch that makes an investor lean in — 31 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 18 — Pitching for Investment
lesson.title_tag: Professor View — Milton Sayegh | Aula 18 — Pitching for Investment | Business English for Luxury Export | Alumni by Better

listenings.text: Hi Milton, it's Karen. I just got off a call with an investor I think you should meet. Her name is Diana, she runs a small fund that backs premium consumer brands, and she's genuinely curious about your business. I gave her the short version, and what really caught her attention was the margin and the repeat clients. She wants a fifteen-minute pitch, nothing fancy, just the story, the numbers, and what you'd do with the money. A word of advice from me: she will push back, hard, but it's not personal, it's just how she tests people. Be ready for one tough question about how scalable this really is. If you can show her the traction is real and the valuation makes sense, I think she'll lean in. I told her you'd send three slides by Friday. You've got this. Call me after and tell me how it went.

Thanks for the time, Diana. Let me keep this simple. We export luxury jewelry from Brazil to boutiques across the United States and Europe. What sets us apart is the margin, it's our craftsmanship and our story that let us price above the market and still sell out. Last year revenue grew by forty percent, and more than half of our buyers came back to order again. That repeat traction is the real proof. Here's the ask. I'm raising one and a half million for a fifteen percent stake, at a valuation of ten million. The money goes into one thing, scaling production so we can say yes to the larger boutiques we keep turning away. It is that bottleneck, not demand, that is holding us back. For a backer who understands premium brands, the return is clear, we have the clients, we have the margin, and now we need the capacity. I'd love to walk you through the numbers.
